summary: Machine policy for automatic app archiving. The local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.admx file explicitly documents the same Appx policy key, AllowAutomaticAppArchiving value name, and app-archiving behavior that the app uses.

setting.name: Automatic App Archiving
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can archive infrequently used apps to save space while keeping them restorable later. This policy decides whether that automatic archiving is allowed on the device.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they want full control over installed apps and do not want Windows archiving apps on its own.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off keeps apps installed and untouched, but it removes an automatic storage-saving behavior. Leaving it at the Windows default lets Windows archive unused apps and lets the user configure the feature.
setting.professional_notes: AppxPackageManager.admx and the ApplicationManagement Policy CSP map AllowAutomaticAppArchiving to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Appx\AllowAutomaticAppArchiving with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows follows its default behavior, periodically checks for infrequently used apps, and lets the user configure the feature.

allowed_values.label: Automatic app archiving disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The system will not archive apps automatically.

allowed_values.label: Automatic app archiving allowed
allowed_values.meaning: Windows can periodically check for and archive infrequently used apps.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AllowAutomaticAppArchiving = 0, which matches the documented disabled state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and newer systems exposing the automatic app archiving policy

states.rationale: The policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: When the policy is not configured, Windows periodically checks for infrequently used apps and the user can control the feature.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that want Windows storage-management defaults
avoid_for: People who do not want Windows archiving apps on its own
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its default app-archiving behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want full manual control over whether apps stay fully installed.
tradeoffs: Windows can archive infrequently used apps.

recommended_profiles.label: No automatic archiving
intended_for: Users who want predictable installed-app state
Managed systems that do not want automatic app archiving
avoid_for: Devices relying on automatic space savings
recommended_profiles.good_when: You do not want Windows archiving infrequently used apps.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want Windows to reclaim some space automatically from unused apps.
tradeoffs: Apps remain installed instead of being archived automatically.
You lose the built-in space-saving behavior for unused apps.

evidence.title: Microsoft ApplicationManagement Policy CSP: AllowAutomaticAppArchiving
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that controls whether Windows can archive infrequently used apps.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps AllowAutomaticAppArchiving to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Appx\AllowAutomaticAppArchiving with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text explains that disabling the policy stops automatic archiving and that not configuring it keeps the default behavior and user control.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes AllowAutomaticAppArchiving = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: The local Microsoft AppxPackageManager.admx file explicitly defines AllowAutomaticAppArchiving under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Appx with valueName AllowAutomaticAppArchiving, and the paired ADML help text states that disabled means the system will not archive any apps while not configured follows the default behavior. The app writes that documented disabled-state value.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same Appx policy key and AllowAutomaticAppArchiving value used by the app. The paired ADML help text states that enabling archives infrequently used apps, disabling prevents app archiving, and not configured follows the default user-configurable behavior.
